name: Spinach Ohitashi (Dashi-Steeped Spinach)
description: Just One Cookbook's Spinach Ohitashi — blanched spinach steeped in a lightly seasoned dashi broth and chilled, topped with bonito flakes (or sesame, for vegan). A clean, refreshing Japanese side that cuts the richness of fried karaage.
recipeIngredient: Katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes) — omit for vegan
recipeInstructions: Combine the water and kombu in a saucepan; slowly bring to a boil, then remove the kombu.
Add the mirin and soy sauce to the dashi; bring to a boil and set aside to cool.
Blanch the spinach in salted boiling water: stem side first for ~20 seconds, then push the leafy part down for 30-45 seconds.
Transfer to an ice bath for 1 minute to cool.
Gently squeeze out the water from the spinach.
Cut into 1 1/2-2 inch pieces and squeeze out any remaining water.
Place the spinach in an airtight container and pour the cooled broth over it.
Refrigerate at least 1 hour (ideally 3-4 hours) until fully submerged and infused.
Serve in small bowls with a little of the broth; garnish with sesame seeds or katsuobushi.
author: Namiko Chen / Just One Cookbook

ai.cliffNotes: Blanch spinach, shock it, steep it in seasoned dashi, chill, and top with bonito flakes — an elegant, make-ahead green that's the perfect cool foil to fried karaage.
keyTips: Blanch stem-side first (the stems need a head start), then push the leaves under — it cooks the bunch evenly so the leaves don't turn to mush.
Shock in ice water and keep it to ~1 minute: the cold bath sets the vivid green and removes much of the oxalic-acid bitterness, but lingering longer leaches out nutrients.
Squeeze the spinach really well (twice — after cutting too) so it soaks up the dashi instead of staying watery and diluting the broth.
It's a make-ahead dish — steep at least 1 hour, ideally 3-4, so the dashi fully infuses; keeps 3 days in the fridge.
Vegan? Use kombu dashi and garnish with toasted sesame seeds instead of bonito flakes.
ai.source: Claude (prose-mined from the Just One Cookbook article)
